metadata.title: Three Questions That Catch Broken Causal Arguments
metadata.purpose: vox-explainer slate cut of Writing Guide candidate 03. Source chapter: writing-guide/chapters/13-reasoning-strategies-improving-critical-thinking.md (cause-and-effect distinctions only: correlation vs. causation, proximate vs. distal, necessary vs. sufficient).
metadata.clock: narration (ElevenLabs) — durations below are word-count estimates until GATE 0 audio lock
metadata.color_semantics: CRIMSON = the collapsed/wrong reading (bad reasoning); TEAL = the correct distinction / diagnostic pass (good reasoning); GOLD = highlighter fill only, used once; SLATE = structural scaffolding. Never swap mid-film.
metadata.note: Card exclusions honored: NO Toulmin model, NO analogy, NO classification, NO comparison, NO problem-solution, NO definition strategy. Stay strictly on causal reasoning and the three distinctions: (1) correlation vs. causation, (2) proximate vs. distal cause, (3) necessary vs. sufficient cause.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

skin_warnings: B01: palette=claude but the cold open is 'un-annotated' — COLD OPEN LAW wants ClaudeComposerAsk
B18: palette=claude but the outro is 'un-annotated' — OUTRO LAW wants ClaudeTitleOutro

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. Ice cream sales and drowning rates both spike every summer. A researcher finds students who had phones banned scored lower on tests that very same year.

card.sub: ice cream sales up. drowning rates up. same spike, same season.

beats.new_visual_element: [TITLE] cold open hook — two correlated facts

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [TITLE] cold open hook — two correlated facts

beats.narration_text: Public discourse treats both of those as cause-and-effect. Neither one is. And the failure is not random — it is three very specific, repeatable mistakes.
beats.scene_description: A newspaper front page, desaturated, with a headline about phone bans and test scores — the kind of story that reports correlation as cause.
beats.image_prompt: B02, newspaper front page with bold headline about smartphones and student test scores, printed broadsheet, photographed on a desk, slightly angled, editorial newsprint aesthetic, desaturated, flat editorial treatment

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [STILL] newspaper — the correlation-as-cause story

beats.narration_text: Why does public discourse keep collapsing three genuinely different things into one? And what does an argument look like once you stop doing that?

card.copy: Three different things — or the same thing?
card.sub: why public discourse keeps collapsing the distinction

beats.new_visual_element: [CARD] the film's question — on screen and in narration

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [CARD] the film's question — on screen and in narration

beats.narration_text: Cause-and-effect is the most common reasoning strategy in policy writing, journalism, and science writing. That makes its failures the most common failures too.

viz.label: a cause-and-effect chain — each item causes the next
viz.mechanic: four horizontal boxes connected by arrows, building left to right on screen; each box labeled with a step in a simple chain (event A causes B, B causes C, C causes D)
viz.note: establish the chain structure before introducing the distinctions

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] the causal chain — four linked steps building

beats.narration_text: The problem: three distinctions that are genuinely different get routinely collapsed into one. Each produces a different kind of wrong argument.

card.sub: each one a different kind of wrong

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [CARD] section — introduces the three-checkpoint structure

beats.narration_text: Distinction one: correlation versus causation. Two things happen together — ice cream and drowning, phones and grades. Co-occurrence is not a mechanism. You need to show how one thing produces the other.

viz.label: co-occurrence vs. mechanism
viz.mechanic: two timeline lines run parallel (ice cream sales, drowning rate); both spike together; a bracket shows they share a third cause (summer heat); the direct arrow between them is drawn in CRIMSON and then struck out — replaced by two TEAL arrows both pointing to the hidden shared cause

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] correlation vs. causation — the spurious arrow struck out

beats.narration_text: The diagnostic question: what is the mechanism? Not just that two things happened together — what is the pathway by which one produces the other?

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [CARD] diagnostic question 1 — mechanism check

beats.narration_text: Distinction two: proximate versus distal cause. In 2015, an oil pipeline ruptured near Refugio State Beach in California. The proximate cause was the rupture itself. A distal cause was the regulatory regime that let an aging pipeline go uninspected.

viz.label: proximate vs. distal cause — the Refugio chain
viz.mechanic: a vertical chain of cause boxes builds top to bottom: regulatory failure (distal, SLATE) -> aging pipeline (SLATE) -> corrosion (SLATE) -> rupture (CRIMSON, proximate) -> spill -> wildlife deaths; brackets label 'proximate' at the rupture node and 'distal' at the top

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] the Refugio chain — proximate vs. distal labeled

beats.narration_text: Both are real causes. Both matter for different arguments. Proximate explains what happened. Distal explains why it was allowed to happen. Conflating them is not an error — it is a choice about which argument to make.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [CARD] diagnostic question 2 — proximate or distal

beats.narration_text: Distinction three: necessary versus sufficient. The pipeline corrosion was necessary for the spill — without it, the pipeline does not rupture. But corrosion alone is not sufficient: a monitored, repaired pipeline can be corroded without rupturing.

viz.label: necessary vs. sufficient — corrosion and the rupture
viz.mechanic: two conditions shown as overlapping regions: corrosion (necessary, shown in SLATE) and no-monitoring (second ingredient, shown in SLATE); their intersection is labeled 'rupture' in CRIMSON; corrosion alone points to a TEAL 'no rupture' outcome when inspected — makes the point that necessary is not sufficient

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] necessary vs. sufficient — the Venn-style condition diagram

beats.narration_text: The diagnostic: is this cause one without which the effect could not happen — necessary? Or is its presence alone enough — sufficient? Many causes are necessary without being sufficient. Claiming otherwise overstates the argument.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [CARD] diagnostic question 3 — necessary or sufficient

beats.narration_text: The writer who can say which kind of cause they are invoking is doing something most journalism does not. Specifying the distinction changes the argument — and it changes which evidence is relevant.
beats.scene_description: A zoomed-in photo of a corroded pipeline section, industrial, slightly rusted, editorial treatment — representing the Refugio spill context.
beats.image_prompt: B12, close-up photograph of a corroded industrial pipeline section, rust visible, industrial setting, slightly desaturated, editorial newsprint treatment, flat print reproduction aesthetic
beats.new_visual_element: [STILL] corroded pipeline — the Refugio implication beat

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [STILL] corroded pipeline — the Refugio implication beat

beats.narration_text: Each failure produces a different kind of wrong argument. Correlation-as-cause overstates certainty. Proximate-for-distal shifts blame. Necessary-for-sufficient inflates a single factor into a complete explanation.

viz.label: the 3x3 matrix: distinction x diagnostic x failure mode
viz.mechanic: a 3x3 grid builds cell by cell: three distinctions across the top (Corr/Cause, Prox/Distal, Nec/Suf), three rows on the left (What collapses, Diagnostic question, Failure mode produced); cells fill in TEAL for correct and CRIMSON for the wrong reading; this is the key visual

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] the 3x3 matrix — all three distinctions and their diagnostics

beats.narration_text: Take this claim: teen depression rose sharply after 2012, the same year smartphone ownership became widespread. Run the three-checkpoint test.

viz.label: the example claim — smartphones and teen depression
viz.mechanic: a large serif quote card shows the claim; below it, three diagnostic question boxes appear one by one (mechanism? proximate or distal? necessary or sufficient?); each box is initially blank

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] the example claim and three blank diagnostic boxes

beats.narration_text: Mechanism: what connects smartphones to depression — social comparison? sleep disruption? displacement of other activities? Each is a different causal story. Proximate: the phone itself, or the attention economy that designed it? Necessary: are phones required for the depression, or one factor among several?

viz.label: the three diagnostic answers fill in
viz.mechanic: the three diagnostic boxes from B14 fill in one at a time with TEAL text answers: (1) mechanism unclear — multiple candidates; (2) proximate=phone, distal=attention-economy design; (3) necessary but not sufficient — one factor among several; each answer lands as the narration names it

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] the three diagnostic boxes fill in — worked example

beats.narration_text: Before you publish a causal claim, run three questions. One: what is the mechanism? Two: am I invoking a proximate or a distal cause — and does my argument need the other kind? Three: is this cause necessary, sufficient, or one factor among several?

viz.label: the three-question checklist
viz.mechanic: three numbered check items appear sequentially in TEAL, each with a short phrase: (1) What is the mechanism? (2) Proximate or distal? (3) Necessary or sufficient?; each item grows in with a FadeIn as narration lands it

beats.narration_text: These are not a critique of cause-and-effect reasoning. They are what lets it work. Each question is a gate. Pass all three and the causal claim holds. Fail one and you know exactly what to fix.

card.copy: Three gates. One clean causal claim.
card.sub: pass all three — the argument holds

beats.new_visual_element: [CARD] the practice payoff — three gates, one clean argument

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [CARD] the practice payoff — three gates, one clean argument

beats.narration_text: Public discourse conflates correlation with causation, proximate with distal cause, and necessary with sufficient cause. Three different failures. Three different fixes. Three questions that take thirty seconds to run.
beats.new_visual_element: [CARD] endcard — question to answer, topic kicker WRITING

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [CARD] endcard — question to answer, topic kicker WRITING
